Hey, welcome to session number two of the Magnetic Email Marketing System and Toolkit. Now in this session you'll discover how to write compelling subject lines that get your emails open including a powerful albeit controversial email subject line strategy that literally compels your prospects to open your emails without fail. We'll also discuss some other things to make sure your emails actually get read. Now Dan, at the break you said you wanted to talk about one other thing which was really important. Well, just going back to offline for email capture, I neglected to list. So one thing is physicality. People come and go through your place of business whether they buy something or they don't buy something, is there an attempt made to capture data? People call, speak to a receptionist, whoever happens to answer the phone, they ask a question they get an answer, they bail out, is there a script, is there an attempt made to capture? Really all offline media can drive to an email capture if that's what you want and you view it as the lowest threshold that you can ask people to go over. I have a client that's doing a very interesting thing right now on radio. Everybody that listens to financial talk will hear all Larry Levin's commercials for stock trading course, futures trading course, etc. And Larry's radio spot offers the trading technique I made $1.9 million worth free and call this 800 number and leave your email address and we'll email to you. That's the whole response and structure. Interesting that we have him calling a toll free recorded message as opposed to hey go to WW. You want the most immediate, when you do a radio you want the most immediate thing you can get, which is phone. But the lowest threshold, so think about what most of that would be. You would call and you would be asked usually by a robot voice to record your name, record it, and people bail out all the time. So the lowest threshold there that he has experimented with quite successfully is somebody calling and just leaving their email address. And then data mining from there. Yeah, and again, so I hope everyone got that. That's a really powerful strategy to test. It's very cool. Toll free recorded message, which still works by the way. So that's actually another great way to capture email address and then take them and then you can email them. Fantastic. Okay, so let's get into one of the things you mentioned was hey, okay, doesn't really deliverability all of that. There's not a lot you can control with that, but you can control the open rate. All right. And so let's talk about subject lines in direct response marketing. Old time we say that the headline for your ad is the ad for the ad. Right. Just talk a little bit about that and subject lines and the importance of them. So a pure sneak up approach. Right. It is really the same as that. It is the same as you say that headline for the ad itself. It is the one's the front, the address side of the postcard. Right. It all gets picked out of the same little harvest of bullet points and headlines that I've written. But the one thing I would say about it that relationship, of course, trumps just about everything. Absolutely. So the catch 22 here is, is that this gets easier when you are emailing to people who want to receive your email, right, and will open your email because it is from you, right? Then a lot of cleverness about subject line becomes unnecessary. So the, the personality, the authority, the relationship, the some level of communication, it is not always asking somebody to buy something. Correct. All of that starts to become important. Right. And we're going to talk about what to put in the body of the email in another segment. But let's go over some, some subject lines here. There are, there is actually more than seven that you can write. But what I've done is these are the ones I rely on the most at GKIC and that I've relied on the most in my career. So let's go over the seven different types of subject lines that you can write to. So the first one I call the intriguing question. And the reason I like the intriguing question is it creates in that open loop in the person's mind. And if the question is good enough, Dan, they want to find out the answer to that question. Talk about that in the, because it's a really powerful psychological technique. Well, so the reason we use questions in advertising rather than overt statements. First of all, one is that you can sometimes bury a claim in a question that you cannot make as an assertive statement. That's right. Right. So do nitrous oxide supplements really replace Viagra? Right. Right. Well, you can ask that as a question. Right. You can't say it. You cannot say it as a claim. But you're still planting it in their head though. Of course you are. Right. Right. It's beautiful. And wanting to know that, may in fact, to your point, have a higher intrigue factor than if I did say it as a statement. Because the reaction to it said as a statement is, oh shit. Right. Right. P.S. Right. The reaction to it stated as a question is, well, who is a doctor saying this? Some new study just come out. What the hell is this all about? Right. So I want to look. Right. So it's used for both of those reasons. It's very powerful. So for example, the subject line that I sent out, the email that I sent out today in your voice, by the way, that you sent out today. It's okay. I never seen them. Yeah, I know. I know. You're safe. So, well, but the subject line was, and this goes to the point that you made just a few minutes ago about the power of relationship. So the subject line was simply the person's first name and the question, what's going on? Dave, what's going on with the question mark from Dan Kennedy? All right. Great open rate on that subject line. Because it was almost like you're pissed at them. All right. Now that doesn't work if you don't have that relationship, but it does work. They want to know what the hell is Dan asking me what's going on. Huge open rate on that type of thing. Let's go to a really, really basic one, which is the standard how to, whatever in doubt, how to works. Why does how to work work so well? Well, to be fair, it doesn't work as well as it did years ago. One of power's old rule about book titles was you could improve just about everything, a one of them by sticking the words how to in front of them. And so thinking about rich automatically becomes better if you made it how to thinking. Given a decline of willingness to do things, it's not as powerful as it once was. So the real variables here are application by audience. Are you talking to do it yourself or are you talking to ambitious individuals? Are you or not? The audience and the thing that the how to is being attached to. But the reason that it works is because it concisely in two words, it makes a specific benefit claim. It says by reading this, you're not going to know about something. You're going to know how to do something. And that's really interesting. That's a really great point about people not, which goes really to the importance of list segmentation, which we're going to talk about later. Because now if you have a segment of your list that our doers, that our proactive that want to do things, that can still work very powerfully. One of my favorites is the next one. I love the secrets. Three secrets, seven secrets, the secret. Here's a really nice one. Seven unusual secrets to better sex. I could not remember for the life of me, the marketer, that all of his things had unusual in it. Do you remember that guy? You know what I'm talking about? Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. That is, and now you're going to get me. I could remember it. You know this whole business with a typewriter and a tent started at George Haleings. George Haleings, that's right. Yeah. Yeah. And George's whole deal was 1,052 unusual somethings. Right. Ways moms are making money from home. Right. 612 unusual. And they were usually big numbers, by the way. George was unique and he didn't use the threes and the sevens and the fourteens. He used 618. Right. I mean, look, the secret. People are very willing to believe that it exists. They've sort of been conditioned to believe it. There's a let them off the hook factor in it. So if you're fat and you know you're fat or you're having crappy sex and you know you're having horrible sex or none at all, or you are broke or any of the conditions that you might cure with information, with medical treatment, with psychotherapy and with whatever. Right. What the existence of secrets does is it immediately relieves you of the responsibility for the condition that you're in. So in again, in a very concise way, it says it's really not your fault, you're fat. Somebody's been ganging up on you and keeping you fat and keeping from you the reason why thin people are thin. It's really not your fault you're broke. People have been keeping from you the secret reason why people are rich. It's really not your fault that you don't seem to attract anybody who is willing to get into bed with you. People have been keeping from you a secret. Now we can sell just about anything. So we can sell pills, we can sell what's the stuff, the smell stuff. Oh, robot therapy. No, no, no. Pheromones. Pheromones. Pheromones. You just don't activate, you're missing a pheromone in your genes that activates the sex impulse. And if you just had this glop, it can be information. It doesn't make any difference. So what it does is it lets the person relieve themselves of responsibility for the problem that they have that you are not going to cure. That's chiefly why it's so popular. It also, of course, arouses curiosity, which is always a good thing. From an open-rate standpoint, curiosity gets the cat to go in private. Yeah, that's exactly right. Before I forget it, even though it's going to be in the next segment where we talk about writing the body of the email, what a lot of people who don't, I don't want to say novices do, is that they answer the question or the secret, whatever it is, too early in the email. So this is like a really big mistake. If you're going to do the secret thing, let's say the secret. You don't want to answer the secret in the first couple of lines of the email, because the point is to get them to read down into the email and keep them engaged throughout the entire process. We're going to be talking more about that, but I don't think I was going to talk about this, so I wanted to mention it. Here's a really powerful one, which gives marketers today, Dan, a huge advantage, which is the current event tie-in. And this one, I also, the subject line here is Kim Kardashian, which also brings in celebrity, but talk about the power of the current thing, the thing that's happening. And in the past, we couldn't do that as marketers. We couldn't act upon something in the news and send out something immediately. To capitalize on it, pre-internet, pre-email, you had to be sure that the current event news story, you had to feel strongly it had legs, that it had a two-week to four-week life cycle. Right? Now, conversely, of course, the internet has helped destroy the life cycle of news events, so there's a circular effect here. But you had to really feel that this thing was going to be a water cooler conversation for two to four weeks because you were going to mail. About the only really current adjustment you could make is to inbound or outbound phone scripts. That's about the only way you could use or mobilizing a sales force to a geographic location. Fire alarm, salespeople mobilized to our house burned down last night and that was in the news. But you now have, with email, you have an opportunity to do it literally on a day-by-day, even an hour-by-hour basis. You can be there right when people are talking about that particular thing. Because news, a current event, is going to be the dominant conversation. It almost doesn't matter. The proportionate seriousness of the event is quite irrelevant. So in the last week, despite people blowing up people and whatever, 30% of all televised newscasts in America, the lead story and the lead tease story has been deflaked. Deflaked, right? The supposedly deflated footballs that made it easier for Brady to throw. Like with the OJ trial, they have moved their way from trying to interview people directly related to that story down to the guy who works at the drive-in window at the McDonald's that Brady stopped at five years ago who might want to comment on this situation. That's right. So this is the story. The show to have trouble gripping his cop. So this is a news event that you could react to immediately and that also has some legs. It's predictable legs. The time period between when it happened and the Super Bowl is going to be its life cycle. So this is the Collier principle. You entered the conversation already occurring in the person's mind. So what's one of the five things that they're thinking about and that they're talking about with buddies, spouse at dinner, coworker, whatever, and you can jump on it, email. In many cases, it does. The final one is a benefit oriented headline, which, you know, pretty much says it all. It's a benefit oriented headline. Well, so are you, are you going to ask me about positive versus negative? We're going to talk about, we're going to talk about that just right now. We were going to get to that just now. So let's talk about that because there's a lot of people disdain this idea, which is that, and here's the truth, folks, negative subject lines almost always that we have found that I have found get a higher open rate than the positive subject line. That's a fact. Okay. It's kind of like the car wreck that you were saying. A lot of people though will say, well, I'm positive and I, I don't want to send out negative sounding subject lines. Let's talk about this because it's a big mistake. It's too size to this coin, right? Benefit or fear, pain, disaster, tragedy, whatever. And you're absolutely right. If you are purely going to play the odds, you will almost always win. But whatever the thing is, the negative, scary, tragic version of it rather than the benefit side of it. Now, some consideration has to be given to where do I go from there? How do I bridge back to being able to sell solutions after I have scared or depressed somebody? But if you are going to play the odds, I mean, I brought, this is an actual split test. So one version, and it's two sides of the same coin, is the end is near of chiropractic practice as you know it. Will your practice survive the crash? The benefit side, a new dawn of a new kind of chiropractic practice. Will you profit from this opportunity? There are two sides of the exact same coin is what they are. So in a split test, the first, the end is near one, out pulls the second 3.5 to one. More importantly, the conversion rate on leads from the second is three times better than the first and the full year value of the customers from the second is seven times better than the first. So if all you looked at was raw numbers, the negative one is the winner. If you track by source all the way through to customer value, the positive one is the winner. So you really have to know what you're trying to accomplish and what you are willing to invest because the positive one suppresses response on the front end. Back to our conversation we had in segment one. Suppresses response. The negative one lifts response. But in terms of 12 month customer value, that whole equation is entire, not wouldn't always be, which also says test. And these are, this is the most easiest test variable there is. Two sides of the same coin. Half the people get email number one, half the people get email number two. It's that simple. Now the only thing you have to be careful about that is if you repetitively test the same variable into the same list, you have to do it in random breakouts. The same group can't get positive all the side and the same side can't get negative. Right. So again, testing is so simple. You just take a portion of your list, you split it up with email. I mean, this stuff is so easy now. It's ridiculous. Which most people, we're going to talk about sloth and things like that in another segment, but most people, they won't even do that. I mean, split it. Take a portion of the list. And as you said, the numbers coming automatically. Your test matrix is when they are done purely online this way. So a list divides in two or three or four and they get a different sub subject line. Right. And maybe first paragraph in order to make the email work. Then each one of those drives them to a site where half CVSL number one, half CVSL. All this has no cost. Right. It has no cost. Right. And the unwillingness, as you say, of people to do it is, is stunning. Absolutely. Absolutely. So again, in the toolkit portion of the program, you get tons of subject lines. And again, the point here is you're not always going to use a negative sounding subject line. You're not always going to use a bizarre sound. You have to mix them up. That's really, really important. We've talked about a negative subject lines. Let's talk about, we need to, we've got it open, but now we need to get it read. Okay. So we've got the email open. We need to get it read. How do we go about getting it read? One of the biggest mistakes I see from our members and from people I've worked with is man, they make it sound like I'm Mr. or Ms. professional and I'm a big corporation, especially the little guys or the small business owner wants to make it sound like he's a big company and it doesn't sound personal. Address that because it's a big problem. Well, it's not just an email mistake. It's a sales copy mistake. Right. And you're right. You see it, you see it as a common mistake. It is in the context of the mistake of being uninteresting and boring. Right. And it is much easier to be uninteresting and boring in a corporate institutional voice than it is in a personal voice. Right. So there's times when you must. There's some companies that I guess have no choice. But other than that, you are almost always going to do better when you are conversationally communicating has one human would to the next. And see readership is about a. Both a sequential agreement. And a constant agreement. And this the person is predisposed to try and not stay with you. They got six million things to do. Email is by its nature disruptive. It's not as bad as a cold telephone call because I clicked open it. But still it is disruptive. Most people let it disrupt their day. Right. So therefore their predilection is how quick can I get rid of this? How quick can I get rid of this? If I was compelled to buy the subject line to open it. But if the first two sentences, I can get rid of it, I'm getting rid of it. Right. Right. And so there's a sequential agreement. I'm going to read some more. I'm going to read some more. I'm going to read some more. And there's a minute by minute word by word line by line. Am I going to read anymore? That's right. Right. So this is not much different than. The almost bygone door to door sales guy. Right. Who his opening line that he said while the chain was still on the door. Had to get the chain released and the door opened a little wider. At least wide enough to shove his foot at it. And so it couldn't be claw. And then he had to have three or four things to say that we'd get him invited in. And that right. It's a great. And so the speaker who comes out on stage has people leaving. Has people burying themselves in their device. So they're leaving even though, you know, physically. Right. See their bodies get up and move anymore. So in every selling situation. Right. There is. Will they stay. And proceed long enough that I can get anything done with this person. And so one very quick way to lose them. Is corporate speak. So if you buy the analogy. That the email is akin to the door to door sales guy is akin to the speaker. Walking out on stage. Okay. Is akin to the first 10 seconds of a television commercial. Okay. If you buy that analogy, then try putting corporate speak in any one of those other environments and see how you do. Right. Send the corporate attorney to the door and have him start with the disclaimer statement and see if he gets the chain off. Right. Right. So and even kind of stuffy corporations. So if you watch Fox business news, I saw the, but the president of Ferrari and the president of Rolls Royce US banner year, by the way, kick ass. I'll take an act on the afternoon, the end of the day show on Fox business news. And neither one of these guys is Mr. Charisma. Right. But he's also not standing there being stuffy and professional as dodgy. He's not a corporate brochure coming out of his mouth. He's a human being having a conversation as best he can with another human being that might be interesting to human beings watching. That's what the email has to be. Yeah. Yeah. Absolutely. So let's go over some elements that you can put in your email and to make it sound more personal, to make it actually interesting. So people want to read it. One of the keys to doing this, as you just mentioned, is to make it personal. And the analogy, even go to big companies, it does have to do with email. But when Wendy's stock was plummeting, who did they roll out? Right. Dave Thomas. Right. Same thing with Lee Iacocca. They made this into personalities. You're reaching back, but you don't have to reach back that far. Okay, go ahead. So when Apple was in the toilet and they had to go beg Steve Jobs to come back. That's right. One, they didn't just put him in a back room and have him fix the company. They put him back out front as a spokesperson, talking to people excitedly about, you know, Bezos goes on TV and does the Barnum drones are going to bring your shoes to you and drop them on your head, nonsense. But he does it because it gets media exposure, it gets publicity, it fascinates people, it affects stock price, et cetera, et cetera. So you don't have to reach back that far. It is a contemporary thing in B2B and B2C that humanity and personality wins. Yeah, absolutely. So here are just some things that you can include in your email. The thing to remember is part of what you're doing is you're selling you, depending on the business. In many cases, you are actually selling you, but you can write about your hobbies. But again, the beauty is if you can tie all of these into a message which is relevant, which we're going to share with you how to do in another segment, you can write about things that are happening in your family. I think a lot of people think, well, no one wants to hear that. My readers don't want to hear that. What would you say to that? Well, so I think there's two things about that. If it is true that your audience has no interest in you, if that actually is true, then A, you have made a whole lot of mistakes. Up to this point. Up to this point. And perpetuating that mistake, rather than digging in and fixing it, is a very costly, at best, dangerous, at worst, mistake. Because the absence of interest in you translates to an absence of preference for doing business with you, which then destroys price and profit margin. So when Warren Buffett talks about a business with a motor around it and not wanting to be invested in businesses that don't have a moat around them, there are different kinds of moats. But one of them is this. So if you reverse engineer backwards, price, premium price, or the ability to even sustain price, profit and the ability to sustain profit, has everything to do with preference for a brand, a place, a person. And that preference, more often than not, has to do with an interest in the person or persons attached to that brand, product or company. It's why nameless, faceless corporate institutions with executives they don't dare put out there go and buy celebrities. The reason they go get them and pay them astronomical sums of money. The reason nationwide insurance has Peyton Manning and a created character, etc. The reason progressive has flow that they created. So they either buy them or they make them is because they don't dare put their CEO or their VP out there, because he will drive people away as if he had to play it. But they know this formula exists. So they have to now dip in their pockets and spend money to make the formula work. So denying the formula is brain dead stupid. You've got to make it work for you one way or another. All right. Um, you got to focus on the people who are responsive and can be reached and can be influenced and can be persuaded and can be kept. So you will very often hear those kinds of complaints. Because if that means you have a big list and you haven't sold anything to them, that means you have been, you have been feeding them for free and you have been entertaining them, informing them, whatever it is you've been doing for them and you've been doing it for free. You are going to create some damage when you change that relationship. That relationship with them. Um, uh, uh, in most cases. So what? Right. Because the ones who run from now being offered an opportunity that costs money, um, uh, are an illusion for the most part anyway. However, if you want to play carefully, you carve out, if it's a big enough list, you carve out only a segment of it and you try different, you try and approach with that segment, you try a different approach with another segment so that you do your damage in a smaller confined and more controlled space. If you have hard addresses, you attempt the sale offline rather than online. So you are not changing the thing they're used to getting. You're only changing the relationship.
